name: Minamoto no Yoshitsune
description: Minamoto no Yoshitsune (1159–1189) was a prominent military commander of the Minamoto clan during the late Heian and early Kamakura periods of Japan. Born Ushiwakamaru, his childhood was marked by tragedy when his father, Minamoto no Yoshitomo, was killed during the Heiji Rebellion. He was subsequently sent to Kurama Temple, ostensibly to become a monk, but secretly trained in swordsmanship and martial arts. He later escaped and sought refuge with Fujiwara no Hidehira in Hiraizumi. Yoshitsune rose to fame during the Genpei War (1180–1185), leading the Minamoto forces to numerous victories against the Taira clan. His strategic brilliance and tactical prowess were instrumental in the Minamoto's triumph, particularly at the battles of Ichi-no-Tani and Dan-no-ura. Despite his crucial role in the Minamoto victory, Yoshitsune's relationship with his elder brother, Minamoto no Yoritomo, deteriorated due to Yoritomo's jealousy and suspicion. Yoritomo eventually turned against Yoshitsune, branding him a traitor and forcing him into exile. Yoshitsune fled back to Hiraizumi, but was eventually betrayed and forced to commit suicide (seppuku) along with his loyal retainers. Yoshitsune is a legendary figure in Japanese history and folklore, celebrated as a brilliant military strategist and a tragic hero. His story has been romanticized in numerous plays, novels, and other works of art, solidifying his place as one of Japan's most enduring historical figures.
subtitle: Tragic Hero of the Genpei War
achievements: Orchestrated the downfall of the Taira clan
Trained in martial arts at Kurama Temple

keyMyths.title: Training at Kurama Temple
keyMyths.description: Yoshitsune, as a young boy named Ushiwakamaru, was sent to Kurama Temple. There he secretly trained in martial arts, honing his skills in swordsmanship and strategy, preparing him for his future role as a military leader.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Ichi-no-Tani
keyMyths.description: Yoshitsune's innovative strategy at Ichi-no-Tani involved a daring descent down a steep cliff, catching the Taira forces by surprise. This audacious maneuver was a key turning point in the Genpei War and cemented Yoshitsune's reputation as a brilliant commander.

keyMyths.title: Death at Koromogawa
keyMyths.description: Betrayed and pursued by his brother, Yoshitsune was cornered at Koromogawa. He chose to commit seppuku rather than be captured, showcasing his unwavering honor and loyalty to his principles, solidifying his image as a tragic hero.
